questions.text: The male reproductive organ of a flower is called:
questions.explanation: The androecium consists of a whorl of stamens.

questions.text: A typical angiosperm anther is ________ and ________.
questions.explanation: Each of the two lobes of the anther contains two pollen sacs (sporangia).

questions.text: The innermost layer of the anther wall which provides nourishment to developing pollen is:
questions.explanation: Tapetum cells are multinucleate and possess dense cytoplasm to nourish microspores.

questions.text: The hard outer layer of pollen grains is called 'exine' and is made of:
questions.explanation: Sporopollenin is one of the most resistant organic materials known.

questions.text: Pollen grains are well preserved as fossils because of the presence of:
questions.explanation: Sporopollenin can withstand high temperatures and strong acids/alkalis.

questions.text: The two cells found in a mature pollen grain are:
questions.explanation: The vegetative cell is larger with abundant food reserves, and the generative cell floats in its cytoplasm.

questions.text: The process of formation of microspores from a pollen mother cell (PMC) through meiosis is:
questions.explanation: Microsporogenesis occurs within the microsporangia of anthers.

questions.text: The ovule is attached to the placenta by a stalk called:
questions.explanation: Funicle is the stalk while Hilum is the point of attachment between body and stalk.

questions.text: The mass of cells enclosed within the integuments of an ovule is called:
questions.explanation: Nucellus cells have abundant reserve food materials.

questions.text: A mature angiosperm embryo sac is ________ nucleate and ________ celled.
questions.explanation: It contains 3 antipodals, 2 synergids, 1 egg cell (all haploid) and 1 large central cell with 2 polar nuclei.

questions.text: Transfer of pollen grains from the anther to the stigma of the same flower is:
questions.explanation: Autogamy is self-pollination within a single flower.

questions.text: Transfer of pollen grains from anther to stigma of another flower of the same plant is:
questions.explanation: Geitonogamy is functionally cross-pollination but genetically similar to autogamy.

questions.text: Flowers which do not open at all are called:
questions.explanation: Cleistogamous flowers ensure seed setting even in the absence of pollinators through autogamy.

questions.text: Pollination by wind is called:
questions.explanation: Anemophilous flowers produce large amounts of light, non-sticky pollen.

questions.text: Which of the following is an example of a water-pollinated plant?
questions.explanation: While lilies and hyacinths grow in water, they are pollinated by insects/wind. Vallisneria uses water currents.

questions.text: The filiform apparatus is found in:
questions.explanation: It guides the entry of the pollen tube into the synergid.

questions.text: Double fertilization involves:
questions.explanation: Unique to angiosperms: one male gamete fuses with egg (zygote), other with polar nuclei (PEN).

questions.text: Triple fusion results in the formation of:
questions.explanation: The fusion of one male gamete with two polar nuclei forms the triploid PEN.

questions.text: Endosperm is consumed by the developing embryo in:
questions.explanation: Non-albuminous seeds lack endosperm at maturity as it is fully utilized during development.

questions.text: The coconut water from tender coconut represents:
questions.explanation: The liquid is free-nuclear endosperm, while the white kernel is cellular endosperm.

questions.text: Remnants of nucellus that are persistent in some seeds like black pepper are called:
questions.explanation: Perisperm is the persistent nucellus used for nourishment.

questions.text: A fruit developed from the ovary without fertilization is called:
questions.explanation: Example: Banana. These fruits are generally seedless.

questions.text: The production of seeds without fertilization is called:
questions.explanation: Apomixis is a form of asexual reproduction that mimics sexual reproduction.

questions.text: Apple and strawberry are examples of 'false fruits' because:
options: They are developed from the thalamus
questions.explanation: False fruits are those where parts other than the ovary (like the thalamus) contribute to fruit formation.

questions.text: The shield-shaped single cotyledon of monocots (like grass) is:
questions.explanation: Scutellum is located towards one side of the embryonal axis.

questions.text: Emasculation is the process of:
options: Removing anthers from a bisexual flower
questions.explanation: Done in artificial hybridization to prevent self-pollination.

questions.text: Which part of the flower attracts insects for pollination?
questions.explanation: Brightly colored petals often produce nectar and scent to attract pollinators.

questions.text: Pollen-pistil interaction is a ________ process.
questions.explanation: It involves continuous dialogue mediated by chemical components of pollen and pistil.

questions.text: Bagging in hybridization experiments prevents:
options: Contamination of stigma with unwanted pollen
questions.explanation: Emasculated flowers are covered with bags (usually made of butter paper).

questions.text: Presence of more than one embryo in a seed is:
questions.explanation: Often seen in Citrus and Mango where nucellar cells start dividing and protrude into the embryo sac.

questions.text: The pollen tube usually enters the ovule through the:
questions.explanation: Micropyle is the small opening in the integuments at the tip of the ovule.

questions.text: In over 60% of angiosperms, pollen grains are shed at the:
questions.explanation: The vegetative cell and generative cell stages.

questions.text: Viability of pollen grains of rice and wheat is lost within:
questions.explanation: Pollen viability varies greatly; some last minutes, others months (like Rosaceae).

questions.text: Cryopreservation of pollen grains is done in liquid nitrogen at:
questions.explanation: Used for long-term storage in pollen banks.

questions.text: Apocarpous gynoecium means:
questions.explanation: Example: Michelia. Fused carpels are called syncarpous.

questions.text: The functional megaspore develops into the:
questions.explanation: Usually, only the chalazal megaspore remains functional.

questions.text: Pollination by animals (like bees, butterflies) is:
questions.explanation: Biotic agents are the most common pollinators in angiosperms.

questions.text: The wall of the fruit developed from the ovary wall is:
questions.explanation: Pericarp can be fleshy (mango) or dry (mustard).

questions.text: Which part of the embryo sac degenerates after fertilization?
questions.explanation: These cells serve specific functions before and during fertilization but are not needed afterward.

questions.text: The entry of the pollen tube into one of the synergids is guided by:
questions.explanation: The filiform apparatus provides a chemical and physical guide.
